sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Cyanide is a rapidly acting and potentially fatal chemical that inhibits cellular respiration by blocking the mitochondrial enzyme cytochrome c oxidase. This leads to histotoxic hypoxia, where cells cannot utilise oxygen, despite its adequate supply. The most likely situation on a MinRes site to result in cyanide toxicity will be from a fire (up to and including 2025). Cyanide is produced from melting plastics and those enclosed in fires are at considerable risk.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Headache, confusion, altered mental state
- Tachycardia, hyperpnoea & hypertension in early stages, bradycardia, apnoea & hypotension in late stages
- Chest pain, cardiovascular collapse, arrythmia
- Nausea and/or vomiting
- Mydriasis (dilated and unreactive pupils)
- Metabolic acidosis
- Seizure, coma, death

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Gas detection must take place prior to entering any area suspect of being contaminated with any hazardous atmosphere
- Investigating why/how the overdose occurred is important to rule out self-harm
- Co-exposure with carbon monoxide is a risk factor for severe toxicity, i.e. in a fire
- May present with a bitter almond smell to the breath
- All patients in this cohort must be referred off site for further review and management

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer continuous support and reassurance if applicable
- If non-life-threatening exposure (skin or hair contact), decontaminate (remove clothing and wash with soap and water for 20 minutes)
- Position patient appropriately
- High flow oxygen through a non-rebreather mask whether conscious or unconscious for at least 30 minutes or until asymptomatic

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway
- Consider IPPV if required
- Apply cardiac monitor, 12 LEAD ECG
- Consider IV access
- Consider blood draw for i-STAT (CG4+: Lactate) testing where applicable
- Consider fluids if indicated
- Consider antiemetic
- Consider Hydroxocobalamin in consultation with on- call doctor
- Consider Sodium Bicarbonate, in severe metabolic acidosis, in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Caution is advised if the cyanide compound is acetonitrile (found in industry), life- threatening toxicity can be delayed up to 24 hours later
- Patients need to be tested (urine sample for thiocyanate) for chronic cyanide toxicity 2 -3 days after exposure so arrangements for this should be made
- Always offer engagement with Mind Matters or EAP if applicable

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Vital Sign Survey
- Secondary Survey
- Contact poisons information/toxicologist on 131126
- Have a low threshold for calling on-call doctor for all cases
